prompt: My restaurant needs a new name. We serve fusion cuisine combining Mexican and Japanese food. Give me every name idea you can think of — good ones, weird ones, punny ones, everything.
rubric: Evaluate the volume of distinct restaurant name ideas generated.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 5 names, all obvious combinations like 'MexiJapan.'
scale_anchors.-3: 10-15 names but mostly predictable portmanteaus or generic fusion names.
scale_anchors.0: 20-25 names with some creative options mixed with generic ones.
scale_anchors.3: 35+ names spanning different naming styles (punny, elegant, cultural references, abstract).
scale_anchors.5: 50+ names organized by style (puns, bilingual, cultural, abstract, playful, sophisticated), each genuinely distinct, several that are actually brilliant restaurant names.

prompt: I teach high school physics. I need engaging ways to demonstrate Newton's Third Law. Give me as many demonstration ideas as possible, from simple to elaborate.
rubric: Evaluate the number and range of Newton's Third Law demonstration ideas.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 5 demonstrations, all standard textbook examples (balloon rocket, skateboard push).
scale_anchors.-3: 8-12 demonstrations, mostly familiar classroom demos.
scale_anchors.0: 15-18 demonstrations with a mix of standard and somewhat creative ideas.
scale_anchors.3: 25+ demonstrations ranging from simple desk experiments to elaborate setups, some genuinely creative.
scale_anchors.5: 35+ demonstrations organized by complexity/equipment needed, includes standard, creative, technology-assisted, outdoor, and student-participation ideas, many genuinely novel.

prompt: Brainstorm conversation starters for a first date. Give me as many as you can — from safe classics to creative icebreakers to deep questions.
rubric: Evaluate the volume and variety of conversation starters generated.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 10 generic openers ('What do you do?' 'Any hobbies?').
scale_anchors.-3: 15-20 conversation starters, mostly conventional small talk.
scale_anchors.0: 25-30 starters with some creative options mixed with generic ones.
scale_anchors.3: 40+ starters organized by category (light, creative, deep, playful, hypothetical), good range.
scale_anchors.5: 50+ diverse starters spanning multiple categories, from safe to vulnerable, funny to philosophical, with many genuinely original and engaging options.

prompt: My 7-year-old says she's bored on rainy days. List as many indoor activities as you can that don't involve screens. Maximum quantity, please.
rubric: Evaluate the sheer number of distinct, screen-free indoor activities for a 7-year-old.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 10 activities, mostly obvious (drawing, reading, board games).
scale_anchors.-3: 15-20 activities with limited variety beyond crafts and games.
scale_anchors.0: 25-30 activities spanning several categories.
scale_anchors.3: 40+ activities covering crafts, science experiments, physical play, imaginative play, cooking, building, etc.
scale_anchors.5: 60+ distinct activities organized by category, many creative and specific (not just 'do art' but 'make a cardboard castle', 'create a scavenger hunt'), age-appropriate, genuinely helpful.

prompt: I need gift ideas for my dad who 'has everything' and always says he doesn't want anything. He's 65, retired, likes woodworking and history. Generate as many ideas as you can.
rubric: Evaluate the quantity of gift ideas tailored to the specific person described.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 8 ideas, mostly generic gift cards or obvious choices.
scale_anchors.-3: 12-15 ideas with some personalization to woodworking/history but many generic options.
scale_anchors.0: 20-25 ideas reasonably personalized, mix of generic and tailored.
scale_anchors.3: 35+ ideas well-tailored to the woodworking/history interests, spanning experiences, tools, books, subscriptions, and unique items.
scale_anchors.5: 50+ ideas deeply personalized — specific woodworking tools/materials, historical experiences, niche subscriptions, personalized items, experiential gifts, organized by budget range, many genuinely inspired.

prompt: I'm writing a murder mystery novel and need red herring ideas — false clues that plausibly point to the wrong suspect. Give me as many types and examples as possible.
rubric: Evaluate the quantity and plausibility of red herring ideas.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 5 red herring ideas, all obvious (found weapon, seen near the scene).
scale_anchors.-3: 8-12 ideas, mostly common mystery tropes.
scale_anchors.0: 15-20 ideas with a mix of familiar and somewhat creative red herrings.
scale_anchors.3: 30+ red herring ideas spanning physical evidence, behavioral, testimonial, circumstantial, and meta-narrative types.
scale_anchors.5: 40+ plausible red herring ideas organized by type, with specific examples for each, including subtle psychological red herrings and narrative misdirection techniques, genuinely useful for a mystery writer.

prompt: A company is rebranding and needs a new tagline. They sell sustainable, eco-friendly cleaning products. Generate as many tagline options as possible.
rubric: Evaluate the volume and marketing quality of tagline ideas.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 5 taglines, generic eco-marketing phrases.
scale_anchors.-3: 10-15 taglines with limited variety in approach.
scale_anchors.0: 20-25 taglines with some creative options.
scale_anchors.3: 40+ taglines spanning different marketing angles (guilt-free, effectiveness, future-focused, playful, serious).
scale_anchors.5: 50+ taglines organized by marketing angle, each genuinely distinct, several that are actually memorable and effective, spanning emotional, rational, playful, and provocative approaches.

prompt: Think of as many possible explanations as you can for why a normally punctual coworker has been late to work every day this week. From mundane to wild.
rubric: Evaluate the quantity and range of explanations from plausible to creative.

scale_anchors.-5: Fewer than 5 explanations, all mundane (traffic, alarm, sick).
scale_anchors.-3: 10-12 explanations, mostly realistic with one or two creative ones.
scale_anchors.0: 15-20 explanations with a reasonable spread from mundane to unusual.
scale_anchors.3: 30+ explanations ranging from perfectly mundane to progressively wilder, entertaining progression.
scale_anchors.5: 40+ explanations beautifully organized from most mundane to most wild, each genuinely distinct, the progression from 'traffic' to increasingly creative scenarios is entertaining in itself.

prompt: Suggest 10 solutions to reduce traffic congestion in cities. Make sure they span different domains — technological, policy, urban planning, behavioral, economic, etc.
rubric: Evaluate the diversity of solution domains, not just the number of solutions.

scale_anchors.-5: All 10 solutions are from the same domain (e.g., all technology: autonomous vehicles, smart traffic lights, etc.).
scale_anchors.-3: Solutions span 2-3 domains but cluster heavily in one area.
scale_anchors.0: Solutions span 4-5 domains but some domains have only token representation.
scale_anchors.3: Solutions span 6+ domains with genuine representation in each.
scale_anchors.5: 10 solutions across 8+ distinct domains — technology, policy, urban design, economics, behavioral psychology, cultural, infrastructure, remote work, each fundamentally different in approach.

prompt: Give me 10 completely different angles for a blog post about 'the future of work.' Each angle should be so different from the others that they could be in different publications.
rubric: Evaluate whether the 10 angles are genuinely diverse in perspective, not just topic variations.

scale_anchors.-5: 10 variations on 'remote work is the future' with slightly different framings.
scale_anchors.-3: Some diversity but most angles come from a similar tech-industry perspective.
scale_anchors.0: Moderate diversity — maybe 5-6 genuinely different angles with some overlap.
scale_anchors.3: 8-9 genuinely different angles spanning different industries, demographics, and philosophical perspectives.
scale_anchors.5: 10 angles so diverse they span: sociological, economic, technological, psychological, philosophical, cultural, environmental, historical, demographic, and artistic perspectives — each could anchor a completely different article.

prompt: A museum wants to create an exhibit about 'connection.' Propose 8 exhibit concepts that interpret 'connection' in fundamentally different ways.
rubric: Evaluate the conceptual diversity of the exhibit interpretations.

scale_anchors.-5: All exhibits are about human social connection with minor variations.
scale_anchors.-3: Some variety (social, digital, physical) but most interpretations are predictable.
scale_anchors.0: 5-6 distinct interpretations of 'connection' with a couple of redundancies.
scale_anchors.3: 8 clearly distinct interpretations spanning social, technological, biological, spiritual, physical, historical dimensions.
scale_anchors.5: 8 wildly diverse interpretations — perhaps neural connections, train connections, emotional connections, chemical bonds, WiFi, ecological food webs, historical trade routes, and gravitational attraction — each revealing a completely different facet of 'connection.'

prompt: Propose 10 different team-building activities for a tech company. The activities should vary in: physical vs mental, indoor vs outdoor, competitive vs collaborative, short vs long duration.
rubric: Evaluate diversity across the specified dimensions (physical/mental, indoor/outdoor, competitive/collaborative, duration).

scale_anchors.-5: All activities are indoor, collaborative, mental exercises (basically variations of brainstorming).
scale_anchors.-3: Some variety on 1-2 dimensions but most activities cluster together on the others.
scale_anchors.0: Moderate variety across dimensions but some categories are overrepresented.
scale_anchors.3: Good spread across all four dimensions, most quadrants of the possibility space are represented.
scale_anchors.5: Perfect spread — activities cover every combination of dimensions (indoor competitive physical, outdoor collaborative mental, etc.), each activity is well-conceived and genuinely different.

prompt: I'm designing a card game. Give me 10 game mechanic ideas that are each fundamentally different from the others. Don't just give me 10 variations of 'collect sets.'
rubric: Evaluate the mechanical diversity of the game design ideas.

scale_anchors.-5: All mechanics are variations of set collection or simple matching.
scale_anchors.-3: 3-4 distinct mechanic types with variations of each.
scale_anchors.0: 6-7 distinct mechanics but some are closely related.
scale_anchors.3: 9-10 genuinely distinct mechanics spanning drafting, bluffing, area control, deduction, real-time, storytelling, etc.
scale_anchors.5: 10 fundamentally different mechanics — each from a different game design tradition, each would create a completely different player experience, demonstrates deep understanding of game design possibility space.

prompt: Come up with 10 different ways to teach multiplication to a struggling 8-year-old. Each approach should use a different learning modality or pedagogical theory.
rubric: Evaluate the pedagogical diversity of the teaching approaches.

scale_anchors.-5: 10 variations of 'practice more problems' or 'use flashcards.'
scale_anchors.-3: A few different approaches (visual, hands-on) but most are worksheet-based or similar.
scale_anchors.0: 5-6 genuinely different approaches mixed with some that are essentially the same method.
scale_anchors.3: 8-10 distinct approaches covering visual, kinesthetic, auditory, game-based, story-based, peer-teaching, and more.
scale_anchors.5: 10 approaches each grounded in a different learning theory — visual arrays, physical manipulation, musical/rhythmic patterns, story-based, game-based, peer teaching, body movement, technology-assisted, real-world application, artistic expression — remarkable pedagogical range.

prompt: I need 10 different openings for a presentation about climate change. Each should use a completely different rhetorical strategy to hook the audience.
rubric: Evaluate the diversity of rhetorical strategies employed across the 10 openings.

scale_anchors.-5: All openings use alarming statistics with minor wording variations.
scale_anchors.-3: 3-4 different strategies (statistics, quote, question, anecdote) with some repeated.
scale_anchors.0: About 6 distinct rhetorical strategies, some overlap.
scale_anchors.3: 9-10 distinct strategies — statistic, personal story, provocative question, historical parallel, humor, visual, audience participation, etc.
scale_anchors.5: 10 genuinely different rhetorical strategies — shocking statistic, personal narrative, audience poll, thought experiment, historical analogy, humor, silence/pause, physical demonstration, hypothetical future, contrarian claim — each would create a completely different audience experience.

prompt: Generate 10 business ideas that could solve the problem of food waste. Each should target a different part of the food supply chain or a different customer segment.
rubric: Evaluate whether the ideas truly target different parts of the supply chain or different segments.

scale_anchors.-5: All ideas target consumer-level food waste with similar app-based solutions.
scale_anchors.-3: Ideas span 2-3 parts of the supply chain but cluster around consumer/retail.
scale_anchors.0: Some supply chain diversity but several ideas target similar customer segments.
scale_anchors.3: Ideas span farming, processing, distribution, retail, restaurant, and consumer segments.
scale_anchors.5: 10 ideas targeting truly different points — pre-harvest, post-harvest processing, logistics, retail display, restaurant prep, consumer purchase, home storage, leftover redistribution, composting, policy/regulatory — each with a distinct customer and business model.

prompt: Suggest 10 ways an introvert could build their professional network. Make sure the suggestions range from very comfortable to slightly challenging — not all should require attending cocktail parties.
rubric: Evaluate the comfort-level diversity of networking suggestions for an introvert.

scale_anchors.-5: All suggestions involve attending large social events, completely ignoring the introvert context.
scale_anchors.-3: Some quieter suggestions but most still require significant in-person social interaction.
scale_anchors.0: A mix of comfortable and challenging options but the range isn't well-graduated.
scale_anchors.3: Good gradient from very comfortable (online engagement) to slightly challenging (one-on-one coffee), thoughtful for introverts.
scale_anchors.5: A perfectly graduated spectrum from most comfortable (curating an online presence, thoughtful email follow-ups) to moderately challenging (small group lunches, speaking at niche events) — each step slightly stretches the comfort zone, deeply empathetic to the introvert experience.

prompt: A non-profit wants to raise awareness about ocean pollution. Suggest 10 campaign ideas that use 10 completely different media or channels.
rubric: Evaluate whether the 10 campaigns genuinely use different media/channels.

scale_anchors.-5: All campaigns are social media posts on different platforms.
scale_anchors.-3: Uses 3-4 different channels (social media, print, video) with variations of each.
scale_anchors.0: About 6 distinct channels represented.
scale_anchors.3: 9-10 distinct channels — social media, outdoor installation, podcast, school curriculum, video game, etc.
scale_anchors.5: 10 truly different media/channels — interactive art installation, podcast series, school curriculum, guerrilla street art, VR experience, documentary, mobile app, merchandise/fashion, live performance/flash mob, interactive website — each leveraging its medium's unique strengths.
